results.possibleIdentity: Norman Solomon was an American painter, photographer, and mail artist who was active in the New York avant-garde scene during the late 1950s and 1960s before later residing in the San Francisco Bay Area. A student at Black Mountain College, he became a close friend and significant collaborator of the artist Ray Johnson. In August 1955, Solomon and Johnson famously coined the term 'moticos' (an anagram of 'osmotic') to describe Johnson’s early small-scale collages and writings. Solomon is also historically significant for capturing the only known on-set production photographs for Jack Smith’s landmark underground film 'Flaming Creatures' (1962); these color images were later published in the 2001 monograph 'On Jack Smith's Flaming Creatures' by J. Hoberman. He was an active participant in the New York Correspondence School and was known by the nickname 'Mr. Postcards' within the mail art community. His association with Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) stems from his connections to the circle of artists, including Remy Charlip and Robert Rauschenberg, who participated in the organization's projects like the Pepsi-Cola Pavilion at Expo '70 in Osaka.

results.possibleIdentity:
Alan Kalker is a Madison, Wisconsin-based artist and furniture maker, known for his brand 'Alan's Fine Woodworking.' His historical identity spans legal, technical, and artistic fields, beginning in the late 1960s and early 1970s in Berkeley, California. In 1969, he was admitted to the bar as a lawyer and served as a representative for Amnesty International at the United Nations in New York. During the early 1970s, he was active in the countercultural and experimental art scene in Berkeley, appearing in the 'Reject's art show' (1971) and being listed in 'The Last Supplement to The Whole Earth Catalog' (1971) in connection with a project titled 'Jerk a Giraffe for Karma.' He also copyrighted an artistic 'Geometrical Model' in 1973.

Kalker's work often bridged the gap between art and technology, a hallmark of the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) movement. He contributed several articles to 'CoEvolution Quarterly' and 'Whole Earth Review' during the 1970s and 1980s, including a 1979 piece on 'Inventors' Services' and early reviews of microcomputer technology. After a career in information technology, Kalker transitioned to full-time woodworking, attending the Center for Furniture Craftsmanship in 2013. His contemporary work includes functional wood sculptures, such as the 'Intimate Light Sculpture' and high-end furniture like benches and wine cabinets, which have been exhibited at the Madison Museum of Contemporary Art’s 'Art Fair on the Square.'
